title: Hisab al-Jummal: Arabic Abjad Numerology — Lesson 12

sections.opening: In the sands of the Arabian desert, when the moon shines among ancient stars, wisdom spanning hundreds of years is transmitted. "Al-ramal tahki qissatak" — the sands tell your story, said the Arab sages as they began developing the sacred Abjad system. After learning about Western life paths, the time has come to dive into the depths of Arabic numerological tradition — Hisab al-Jummal. This system is not merely a calculation method; it is a bridge between your name and the sacred spirit, between letters and the soul. The Arabs understood something profound: every letter carries ancient wisdom in its essence, and every name is a spiritual map guiding its bearer through the world.
sections.theoryA:
What is Arabic Abjad Numerology and How Does It Work?

Abjad numerology (Hisab al-Jummal) is an ancient Arabic numerological system based on the numerical values of the Arabic alphabet. The system operates according to the original order "Abjad Hawwaz Hutti Kalaman Sa'fas Qarshat Thakhadh Dhadhagh" and assigns each letter a specific numerical value.

The letter Alif (ا) opens with the number 1, Ba (ب) carries the value 2, Jim (ج) receives 3, and so forth until Ghain (غ) which reaches the value 1000. The system was constructed so that the first letters carry units (1-10), the second carry tens (20-90), the third carry hundreds (100-900), and the fourth carry thousands. This is a harmonic structure that parallels the Arabic cosmic structure.

In Abjad calculation, every name is calculated by adding the values of its constituent letters. For example, the name "Fatima" (فاطمة): Fa (ف) = 80, Alif (ا) = 1, Ta (ط) = 9, Mim (م) = 40, Ha (ة) = 5. Total: 135. This value is reduced to a single digit (1+3+5 = 9) and becomes the person's life path.

What's special about the Abjad system is its connection to Quranic numerology. Words like "Allah" (66), "Muhammad" (92), and the great "Bismillah" (786) carry significant numerical values. When a connection is found between a person's name and a sacred word with the same numerical value, this is seen as a sign of special spiritual destiny.

practice1.instruction: Calculate the Abjad value of your first name in English, comparing it to the Arabic alphabet (use the table: A=1, B=2, C=3... up to Z=26). Add the values, reduce to a single digit, and compare to your life path from the Western system.

sections.theoryB:
How Does Abjad Numerology Connect to Other Traditions?

The connection between Abjad and Jewish Kabbalah is deep and fascinating. Both systems are based on the idea that letters are cosmic creative forces. While Hebrew Gematria relies on 22 foundational letters, Arabic Abjad is based on 28 letters that correspond to lunar cycles. This creates resonance with astrology - the 28 days of the lunar cycle parallel the different energies of the letters.

In Tarot, we see a fascinating connection to the Minor Arcana. The 28 letters of Abjad divide into four groups (fire, water, air, earth) exactly like the suits in Tarot. The first letters (1-7) correspond to the power of fire, the second (8-14) to water, the third (15-21) to air, and the fourth (22-28) to earth. This allows for deep readings that combine the power of the name with elemental energies.

The number 786 ("Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim") parallels the number of different ways of the 22 Major Arcana in Tarot (22×36-6). In the stellar system as well, we see the same pattern: the first letters correspond to the first three zodiac signs (Aries, Taurus, Gemini), the second to the next three, and so on.

In Kabbalah, Arabic letters correspond with the Sefirot through Gematria. For example, the value of "Nur" (light) = 256 connects to the number of Sefirot and paths in the Tree of Life multiplied by itself. This connection shows that divine wisdom flows through all sacred languages.

practice2.instruction: Choose an Arabic word from the table (like "Nur" or "Hubb") and calculate its Abjad value. Then find a Tarot arcana with a corresponding or close number. Write how the energy of the word connects to the card's meaning.

sections.deepDive:
The Secret Behind the Arabic Abjad System

What's not written in most books is that the Abjad system originates from the mystical tradition of "Ilm al-Huruf" - the science of letters. Ibn Arabi, the great mystic, taught that every Arabic letter is a gateway to divine wisdom. The letter Alif, for example, represents primordial unity, while the letter Mim (40) connects to the forty days of prophetic seclusion.

The deeper secret lies in the connection to nature. Ancient Bedouins discovered that Abjad values correspond to the vibrational frequencies of desert sand and wind. Therefore, important names in Arabic always carry values divisible by 7 (the number of heavens) or 11 (the number of spiritual revelations).

Moreover, the final letters (ت ث خ ذ ض ظ غ) carry the highest values because they represent boundaries - the place where the known meets the unknown. This is why in Abjad calculation, names of people with high spiritual destiny often contain these letters.

The deepest point: the tradition teaches that when two people are born with the same Abjad value, their souls belong to the same "spiritual family" in the universe. This isn't about reincarnation, but about a shared spiritual source expressed in the vibrational power of the name.

questions.question: What is the numerical value of the letter Alif (ا) in the Abjad system?
questions.explanation: The letter Alif opens the Abjad system with the value 1, representing primordial unity.

questions.question: How is the name 'Muhammad' (محمد) calculated in the Abjad system?
questions.explanation: Mim (40) + Ha (8) + Mim (40) + Dal (4) = 92, the traditional value of the name.

questions.question: How many foundational letters are there in the Arabic alphabet for the Abjad system?
questions.explanation: 28 foundational letters, corresponding to the 28-day lunar cycle and division into four elements.

questions.question: What is the famous numerical value of 'Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim'?
questions.explanation: 786 is the famous Abjad value of the phrase 'Bismillah ar-Rahman ar-Rahim', considered sacred in Islamic tradition.

questions.question: What is the Arabic name for the science of letters and numbers?
questions.explanation: Ilm al-Huruf (علم الحروف) is the Arabic name for the science of letters, used by Ibn Arabi and other mystics.

closing.summary: We learned about Hisab al-Jummal, the Arabic numerological system that connects the letters of a name to spiritual wisdom. We saw how the 28 Arabic letters carry numerical values that connect to the lunar cycle and the four elements. We discovered connections to Kabbalah, Tarot, and astrology, and how sacred words like 'Allah' and 'Bismillah' carry deep numerological meaning. We practiced calculating names and understanding connections to other numerological systems.
closing.wisdom: Huruf ismak tahmil sirran azim - the letters of your name carry a great secret.
closing.nextPreview: In the next lesson, we'll dive into the numerology of special dates - how to calculate and interpret important occasions in your life.

faq.question: Can Abjad be used with non-Arabic names?
faq.answer: Yes, names can be converted to Arabic letters or use numerical value equivalents. The important thing is the intention and respect for the tradition.

faq.question: What's the difference between Abjad and Hebrew Gematria?
faq.answer: Abjad is based on 28 letters and connects to the lunar cycle, while Hebrew Gematria is based on 22 letters and connects to Tarot arcana and the Kabbalistic Tree of Life.

faq.question: Is it permissible to use Abjad for fortune-telling?
faq.answer: In strict Islamic tradition, fortune-telling is considered forbidden. Abjad is intended for spiritual understanding and contemplation, not predictions.

faq.question: How do you handle special letters like Ta Marbuta (ة)?
faq.answer: Ta Marbuta is counted as a regular Ha (value 5), and Alif Maqsura (ى) is counted as a regular Ya (value 10).
